Details:
Reduction of perchlorate by biological filtration can be very effective (exceeding 99 perecent) under anerobic conditions (removal below detection limit of 4 ppb) and is regulated by presence of oxygen, molybdate, and to some extent nitrate. Biological perchlorate reduction technologies such as packed bed reactor uses different filter media such as GAC, sand, diatomaeous earth, with perchlorate-reducing bacteria to reduce perchlorate from the drinking water.
Microbial reduction is attractive because the hazardous perchlorate is converted to two innocuous compounds, chloride and oxygen. Acetate, ethanol, lactate, methanol, sulfur, and hydrogen gas are suitable electron donor for perchlorate-reducing bacteria and they play important role in effective biochemical reduction of perchlorate. Microbial reduction technology may require high monitoring and maintenance and are difficult to implement in existing facility with high output. The specific issues with this treatment option include sustainability; stability; the multiple barrier concept; potential pathogen release; distribution system impacts; and utility, public, and primacy agency acceptance.
 
Anaerobic biological treatment unit processes present challenges because they are not currently used to treat drinking water in the United States. Additionally, these processes require the control of any potential microbial, nutrient, and electron donor releases into the distribution system.
